text: This video depicts our operative approach to the rectal advancement flap for the treatment of symptomatic fistula in ANO. The patient was a fifty-six year old male with a ten year history of a fistula which periodically drained pus. The diagnosis was confirmed two months before definitive surgery by examination under anaesthesia, at which time a non-cutting ceton was placed.

The surgeon defines the anatomy of the fistula, confirming a transphincteric fistula involving fifty percent of the sphincter complex. The draining ceton is removed, and the internal opening is excised.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to make a circumferential incision around the internal opening, followed by sharp dissection to excise the opening. Mucosal flaps and a small cuff of internal sphincter are raised above and below the opening, avoiding excess charring. Haemostasis is achieved, and the wound is irrigated. The internal opening is closed with a series of interrupted two polysorb sutures to secure the defect.
The surgeon removes the Hill Ferguson retractor and sequentially ties the sutures to secure the closed internal opening.

The surgeon reinserts the retractor and cuts the sutures. The edges of the rectal advancement flap are opposed using a series of interrupted tuopolysorb sutures. Care is taken to avoid excess tension on the flap, with generous bites of tissue ensuring good apposition of the anoderm and rectal mucosa while minimizing dead space beneath the flap.

The surgeon removes the retractor and sequentially ties the sutures. The retractor is reinserted, and the surgical area is irrigated. Defects in the flap are identified, and additional sutures are placed to ensure proper closure.

The surgeon displays the external fistula opening to prevent recurrence of an abscess or fistula, ensuring good hemostasis and avoiding flap tension and ischemia. Generous bites of tissue are taken to avoid dead space.
